title: Tool Output Lies — Five Lessons on Not Trusting the Error
body:
A clean two-step AD box (description-field cred -> LAPS reader -> DC) teaches less about AD than it does about TRUSTING TOOL OUTPUT. Every wall on that box was a tool lying, and the skill that finishes boxes is knowing when to disbelieve the screen.

1. A FAILURE FROM ONE TOOL IS NOT A FACT. nxc returned STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE on the LAPS local-admin password — then impacket-psexec walked in with the SAME creds. nxc had defaulted to a domain context; the account was local. Lesson: when creds 'fail,' try a second tool and a second auth mode before re-looting. One red line is a data point, not a verdict.

2. AUTOMATED ENUM OVER-CLAIMS. winPEAS reported GenericAll on Domain Admins, the domain root, the DC computer object — 69 objects. If true, you'd skip LAPS and just add yourself to DA. You couldn't, because it WASN'T true — winPEAS mis-attributed ACLs running as the service account and threw null-refs mid-scan. Lesson: if an enum tool hands you a path that's too good (DA in one step), the burden of proof is on the tool. Validate with PowerView/BloodHound before you believe it — and never put it in a report unverified.

3. AN AUTH ERROR OFTEN ENCODES A GROUP MEMBERSHIP. evil-winrm gave WinRMAuthorizationError with valid creds — that's not 'wrong password,' it's 'not in Remote Management Users.' The creds were fine; WinRM just wasn't the door. Lesson: read the SPECIFIC error. Authorization != authentication. Don't burn 20 minutes re-checking a password the error already told you was correct.

4. THE TASK ISN'T THE SHELL — IT'S THE CAPABILITY. The instinct was 'get a shell as the LAPS reader.' But reading LAPS is pure LDAP. The reader didn't need WinRM, didn't need a shell, didn't need to be pivoted to interactively. Lesson: ask what the principal can DO, not how to log in as them. Often the capability is one query away and the shell is a detour.

5. MATCH THE PAYLOAD TO THE CATCHER. A meterpreter reverse_tcp caught with plain nc = a dead shell that connects and hangs (nc can't speak the staged meterpreter protocol). Lesson: nc catches shell_reverse_tcp; meterpreter needs multi/handler. A 'connected but dead' shell is almost always a payload/handler mismatch, not a broken target.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# The reflexes these lessons should build
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. Creds 'failed'? Second tool, second auth mode, before re-looting:
nxc smb $ip -u USER -p 'PW'                 # domain context
nxc smb $ip -u USER -p 'PW' --local-auth    # local context
impacket-psexec USER:'PW'@$ip               # different code path entirely

# 2. Enum tool claims a god-mode ACL? Verify before you believe (or report) it:
Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity 'Domain Admins' -ResolveGUIDs |
  ? { $_.SecurityIdentifier -eq (ConvertTo-SID $env:USERNAME) }

# 3. Read the SPECIFIC auth error:
#   WinRMAuthorizationError  -> valid creds, NOT in Remote Management Users -> pivot to LDAP/SMB
#   STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE     -> wrong creds OR wrong context (try --local-auth)
#   STATUS_ACCOUNT_RESTRICTION-> creds fine, logon-type/time/host restricted

# 4. Ask 'what can this principal DO' — many capabilities need no shell:
#   read LAPS, DCSync, read a GMSA password, kerberoast = all pure LDAP/RPC

# 5. Payload <-> catcher pairing:
#   shell_reverse_tcp  -> nc -nlvp PORT
#   meterpreter/*      -> msfconsole multi/handler (matching payload)
warn: These are anti-patterns to your own tooling, not the target's. The failure mode they prevent is the expensive one: hours spent re-deriving a credential or chasing a vuln that a tool hallucinated. When stuck, the first question is often 'is the tool lying to me?' not 'what else can I run?'

choices.label: Apply it — LAPS reader privesc walkthrough
Apply it — hunt creds in LDAP description fields
Verify an ACL claim properly — ACL abuse
Reset methodology on a new box
